text.title: OKR Setting Process
text.summary: Six-step process from strategy alignment through weekly tracking cadence.

rule.statement: Before drafting, gather: company mission/vision, annual goals, previous period retrospective findings, and current market conditions. Ask: "What must we accomplish this period to move toward our vision?"
rule.rationale: OKRs disconnected from strategy are just metrics. The upward connection is what makes them directional rather than operational.

rule.statement: Brainstorm answers to three questions: "What is most important right now?", "If we could only achieve one thing, what would it be?", "What would make this quarter a success?" Then cut to 3–5 Objectives maximum.
rule.rationale: Starting from one-thing thinking forces prioritisation before phrasing. This prevents the common failure of treating every theme as equally urgent.

rule.statement: For each Objective, answer three questions: "How will we know we achieved it?", "What metrics matter?", "What is the target number?" Test: can you answer with just a number at the end of the period?
rule.rationale: If you cannot answer with a single number, the KR is still a task or a vague aspiration. The number test is the falsifiability test.

rule.statement: Run four checks before publishing: Objectives are inspiring and qualitative, KRs are measurable outcomes not tasks, 70% confidence on each KR, all connect to higher-level goals, total count respects the limit.
rule.rationale: Skipping validation produces OKRs that look correct but fail in practice. The most common failure is KRs that are tasks dressed up with numbers.

rule.statement: Share OKRs with all stakeholders, get explicit buy-in, and make them visible — dashboard, wiki, or team channel. Visibility without commitment produces OKRs that are set and forgotten.
rule.rationale: OKRs only work as coordination tools when everyone knows and accepts them. Private OKRs are just private goals.

rule.statement: Run weekly check-ins with progress percentage and confidence level per KR. Score monthly. Hold an end-of-period retrospective focused on accuracy, not just achievement.
rule.rationale: Weekly check-ins surface blockers early. Monthly scoring maintains accountability without micromanagement. Retrospective on accuracy (not just score) improves the OKR-setting process itself.
